text: Prompt caching sounds like infrastructure trivia, but it is not trivia.
For long-running agents, cache discipline is the line between a usable product and an expensive demo.
At Claude Code, cache hit rate is monitored like uptime, and drops are treated like incidents.
So prompt caching is not tuning; it is an architecture constraint.
Prompt caching is prefix matching from the first token.
The good ordering is static first, dynamic last.
That ordering creates a large shared prefix across sessions.
Bad ordering pushes volatile fields into the front and destroys reuse.
Rule card: put static blocks first and keep dynamic tails at the end.
The scary part is fragility: harmless-looking changes break cache.
A timestamp in the static prefix can invalidate every request.
Non-deterministic tool ordering also creates misses.
Changing tool parameters or schemas mid-session does the same.
Use guardrails: deterministic ordering, frozen static blocks, explicit change lanes.
When state changes, avoid rewriting the system prompt.
Send updates as messages on the next turn instead.
Tool-set churn mid-session is another cache killer.
Plan Mode should be behavior toggles, not tool catalog swaps.
For huge MCP catalogs, defer-load full schemas behind stable stubs.
Result: stable prefix with dynamic capabilities.
Prompt caches are model-specific.
Late Opus-to-Haiku switching can increase total cost.
A subagent handoff is safer because context is compressed before routing.
Rule of thumb: switch models early, or hand off with compact context.
Compaction starts when the context window is near full.
Naive summarization with a separate prompt and tools loses cache immediately.
That divergence starts at token one and cost spikes.
Cache-safe forking reuses the exact parent prefix.
Reserve a compaction buffer for prompt and output tokens.
Outcome: only the new tail is uncached, so cost and latency stay controlled.
Run prompt caching with five operating rules.
Those rules improve both latency and cost in production.
Start implementation with a concrete checklist.
Final line: design around prefix stability from day one.
